issues.title: Cross‑Site Scripting (XSS) risk when rendering task/title/description
issues.description: The app accepts and stores user-provided text (title, description, project names) and later renders it into the DOM. If values are not always escaped or sanitized (especially if a future feature allows any HTML/rich text), an attacker or malicious payload could inject script/markup that executes in the app context. Because the app persists data locally (IndexedDB/localStorage), an XSS can become persistent and allow reading/writing stored data, modifying the UI, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
issues.recommendation: Treat all user content as untrusted. For MVP keep descriptions and titles strictly plain text and always escape/encode before inserting into the DOM (never use innerHTML). If you add rich text later, use a vetted sanitizer library (e.g., DOMPurify) with a strict allowlist and canonicalize input. Apply a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) that disallows inline scripts and only allows scripts from trusted origins. Include tests to verify that attempted script payloads render as escaped text and do not execute.

issues.title: Sensitive data at rest — unencrypted local storage
issues.description: All user data is stored unencrypted in the browser (IndexedDB or localStorage). If a device or browser profile is compromised, theft occurs, or browser sync is enabled, sensitive personal data (task titles, notes, etc.) can be exposed or uploaded to cloud backups.
issues.recommendation: Document the risk in the README/Settings and offer an optional local encryption feature for users who want protection: use the Web Crypto API with a user-supplied passphrase to encrypt payloads before persisting to IndexedDB. Derive keys with PBKDF2 or scrypt/Argon2 (via a well-reviewed lib) and encrypt with AES‑GCM. Clearly explain limitations (lost password = lost data, encryption only local) and do not store passphrases. Also educate users about browser profile sync and backups that may upload data to cloud providers.

issues.title: Fallback to localStorage increases attack surface and persistence issues
issues.description: localStorage is synchronous, accessible from any script on the origin, and more likely to be read by third‑party scripts injected into the page (e.g., via compromised third‑party libraries or misconfigured CSP). Using localStorage as a fallback could make sensitive task data trivially accessible to injected scripts and complicate data integrity compared to IndexedDB.
issues.recommendation: Only use localStorage as a last-resort fallback and limit the amount/type of data stored there. Prefer IndexedDB for data storage. If localStorage must be used, store only non-sensitive, minimal metadata. Enforce a strict CSP and avoid loading any third-party scripts that could access the origin storage. Consider encrypting data even when stored in localStorage if sensitive.

issues.title: Dependency and supply‑chain risks
issues.description: The specification calls for small dependencies (React, idb, etc.), but even small packages can contain vulnerabilities or be the subject of supply‑chain attacks. Unpinned or automatically updated dependencies can introduce malicious code or regressions.
issues.recommendation: Pin dependency versions in package.json and commit the lockfile. Enable automated dependency scanning (Dependabot, Renovate) and CI checks (npm audit, Snyk) for known vulnerabilities. Add a CI job that fails builds on critical/high vulnerabilities. Keep third‑party usage minimal and review new dependencies before adding. Consider verifying package signatures where supported.

issues.title: Secrets management in CI / deployments
issues.description: The build/deploy pipeline (GitHub Actions) will require deploy tokens/credentials for hosting providers. If these secrets are misconfigured, leaked in build logs, or committed into the repo, they can be abused to tamper with production assets or leak code.
issues.recommendation: Never commit secrets or tokens to the repository. Store deploy credentials in GitHub Actions Secrets (or equivalent) and restrict access to only required actions. Do not echo secrets to logs. Use least privilege tokens (e.g., deploy-only tokens). Add scanning to CI to detect accidental secrets in commits and enable branch protection. Rotate tokens periodically.

issues.title: Weak input validation and potential prototype pollution / unauthorized field updates
issues.description: Service functions accept Partial<TaskFields> and merge updates into stored objects. If input is not validated/whitelisted, attackers could inject properties like __proto__ or constructor fields, causing prototype pollution or corruption of app state. Additionally malformed data types (e.g., strings instead of booleans) may break logic.
issues.recommendation: Validate and whitelist fields accepted by update APIs. Use a schema validator (zod, Joi, or TypeScript runtime checks) to enforce types and permitted keys. When merging updates, do not use naive deep merges that include prototype properties; use safe cloning and explicit assignment of allowed properties. Add unit tests that assert malicious keys and unexpected types are rejected.

issues.title: Lack of CSP / security headers on static hosting
issues.description: The spec mentions serving over HTTPS and CSP but does not prescribe specific headers. Without a strict CSP, scripts injected via third‑party libs or browser extensions can run and access storage. Missing HTTP security headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security) increase risk of clickjacking, data leakage, or man-in-the-middle.
issues.recommendation: Configure the hosting provider to serve a strict Content-Security-Policy that disables inline scripts (use nonces/hashes if necessary) and only allows scripts from trusted origins. Add X-Frame-Options: DENY or CSP frame-ancestors. Enable Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS). Use Subresource Integrity (SRI) for any external resources and set conservative Referrer-Policy and Feature-Policy/Permissions-Policy headers as appropriate.

issues.title: Service worker / caching pitfalls
issues.description: If a service worker or aggressive caching is later added (or host config misconfigured), stale or malicious cached assets could be served. Improperly cached index.html can prevent updates; while a compromised asset could persist and access local data.
issues.recommendation: If you add a service worker, follow best practices: use a safe update strategy (prompt users or skipWaiting only with care), validate assets, and keep logic simple. Ensure index.html is served with short TTL and static assets with content-hashed filenames have long TTLs. Include SRI and strong CSP to reduce risk of tampered assets. Add automated tests that confirm new deploys are used after update.

issues.title: Privacy and inadvertent cloud sync / backups
issues.description: Browser profiles often have sync enabled (Chrome/Firefox), which can upload local storage and IndexedDB data to cloud accounts. Users may assume 'local-only' means data never leaves their device, but browser sync or OS backups can cause uploads.
issues.recommendation: Update docs and UI to clearly explain that storage is local to the browser profile but may be synced or backed up depending on user settings. Provide an in-app Privacy/Settings note recommending users disable browser sync for the profile or enable local encryption if they do not want data uploaded. Consider detecting when Profile Sync is enabled and warn users.

issues.title: Insufficient error handling / information leakage in error messages
issues.description: Service/Storage errors may surface stack traces or internal details to users (or logs) which could reveal implementation details that facilitate attacks.
issues.recommendation: Ensure service functions return meaningful but opaque error messages to the UI; log detailed errors only to developer-only logs (CI or local dev) but never expose stack traces or internal DB schemas to end users. Sanitize any error information that might be shown in production. Add tests verifying error cases surface user-friendly strings.
